text: Tabor discusses Enochian Judaism mysticism in the Dead Sea Scrolls as providing context for understanding Paul's mystical theology, noting parallels in language of exaltation and cosmic transformation ("I am sitting among the gods I've been exalted I'm above all the Elohim")—relevant to understanding the Hellenistic-Jewish milieu of the Second Temple period.
quote: the Dead Sea Scrolls were not even discovered and that's where we really find some of the mysticism particularly in the teacher hymns...he talks about I am sitting among the gods I've been exalted I'm above all the Elohim

text: Jesus's divine form in Philippians 2 is cited as evidence of quasi-divine status, reinforcing the understanding of Jesus within Hellenistic theological categories rather than purely Jewish messianic frameworks.
quote: He has the form of a god in Philippians 2 and then he comes down and takes on the form of a slave

text: Taylor notes Paul's theological framework prioritized the heavenly "son of God" Messiah identity, with Davidic descent being incidental to his theology, consistent with the book's discussion of "Son of God" as the dominant theological title in early Christianity.
quote: Paul very much goes for the more heavenly Messiah, the son of God, you know, the revelation of the son is what counts for him

text: The Philippians Hymn (pre-literary tradition in Paul) depicts Christ as a divine being ("in the form of God") who "emptied himself taking on the form of a slave" and was crucified, then "God highly exalted him" to supremacy over all creation—demonstrating early Christians conceptualized Jesus's exaltation within Greco-Roman institutional frameworks of divine beings assuming human form and being subsequently exalted.
quote: Christ who although he was in the form of God...emptied himself taking on the form of a slave and coming in the likeness of humans...God highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name

text: McClellan cites Paul Holiday's commentary to argue that Philippians 2's hymn reflects the Hellenistic conceptual template of the "name-bearing angel," showing Jesus's exaltation and divine status are expressed through Greco-Jewish frameworks.
quote: particularly the hymn in Philippians 2 seems to reflect that idea of Jesus um not as an angel but taking that conceptual template of the name bearing Angel and saying Jesus is doing the same thing

text: Scholarly analysis of Philippians 2 (Boers, Perryman, Holloway, Fredrickson) interprets "form of God" as Jesus filling a functional role or divine appearance rather than sharing essence. This functional understanding of divine titles aligns with Hellenistic royal and mediatorial frameworks.
quote: Scholars are in pretty widespread agreement that this does not mean that Jesus shared one Essence with God but just that Jesus had the outward appearance or filled the role of a deity the way that God would

text: John taught a universal kingdom message that transcends particularistic identity: "the whole point of being jewish or israelite is to witness and be a light to the nations," positioning the kingdom as an inclusive, institutionally expansive vision rather than tribal or sectarian in nature.
quote: john is teaching a universal message of the kingdom of god that's going to fill the earth remember in the prophecies all nations come to jerusalem and learn the way of the lord

text: Stanley notes that Asclepius, the Greek healing god, received titles and veneration closely parallel to early Christian language about Jesus—"divine Savior," "loving Lord and Master," "the one who gives life"—suggesting the Greco-Roman religious idiom for divine titles.
quote: he is they have hymns to him as the divine Savior the loving Lord and Master uh and um you know the the one who gives life and all these kinds of things
